narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72.9% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your placement in the lowest decile for grades in your first and third undergraduate years, which stood out starkly compared to the much higher average deciles—around the sixth—of students who went on to pass the bar. These early and later undergrad performance indicators were the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as they suggested challenges in sustaining the rigorous academic pace needed for law school and the bar. Your LSAT score of 35, while respectable, fell below the average of 37.27 for those who passed, but it actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward success, softening the overall risk assessment. Your upper-middle family income aligns well with many successful bar takers, and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm for passers, so those elements didn't raise red flags. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average for those who passed, offering some reassurance but not enough to offset the decile concerns. In essence, the model saw your profile as riskier than typical accepted students, whose stronger year-in, year-out grade rankings signal better odds of bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven estimate based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive influence or strengthening your academic profile through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile standings. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, as many students in similar positions have turned things around with focused effort. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback on reapplying, and remember, one model's view doesn't define your path—plenty of determined students like you succeed by addressing these key areas head-on. With strategic steps, you can reshape your story for the next cycle.
